01
About

We investigate crimes against humanity by state and corporate actors

Parallax uses spatial and open-source methods to transform complex investigations into strategic tools for the defence of human rights, bridging grassroots organisations and legal experts to disrupt systemic power imbalances in international law.

A collective of:
a.Open-source investigators
b.Legal researchers
parallax /ˈparəlaks/ · noun

The apparent displacement, or difference of position, of an object, as seen from two different stations or points of view. — astron., opt.

We take the word as a method. For every official account of what happened, there is another line of sight: from the ground, from the margins, from the communities who bear the cost. What looks definitive from one angle rarely looks definitive from two.

Counter-investigation is the discipline of insisting on the second view.

02
Current investigation · Active

Crimes Against Humanity at the UK–France-Belgium Border

REGION
UK / FR / BE
PERIOD
1990s – ONGOING
ENGLISH CHANNEL / LA MANCHE · STRAIT OF DOVER

Our pilot project investigates violence against refugees and people seeking asylum attempting to reach safe haven in the UK via the English Channel: border violence that stems from hostile Government policies criminalising migration.

Hostile policies are sustained by a rhetoric that frames people seeking safety as a “threat”, and the law, selectively applied, provides the State cover. This is a clear example of how the UK ignores or invokes international humanitarian obligations when it suits its interests.

Our investigation is both backward and forward-looking: tracing this violence historically, in some cases as far back as the 1990s, while documenting ongoing harm and anticipating the consequences of policies still being written.

To restore that imbalance, we collect and analyse data to identify patterns of harm: forced evictions, interceptions of boat launches, the deployment of tear gas, rubber bullets and other less-than-lethal weapons, deliberate interventions in the environment and architecture of Northern France and the deaths that follow.

We believe these patterns are structural, widespread and systematic: the product of policies that have created and continue to create injury, discrimination, displacement and death for people seeking asylum. Taken together, we believe these actions amount to crimes against humanity committed against displaced people along the UK–France–Belgium border.

Objectives
01

Evidence

Develop evidence to the highest digital standards.

02

Legal Accountability

Pursue accountability through international jurisdiction.

03

Public Awareness

Raise awareness amongst the public about ongoing state violence.

03
Network

Organisations we work alongside, and coalitions we are part of, to share knowledge, build evidence and pursue accountability.

PARTNER
Human Rights Observers
Calais / Grande-Synthe, France

HRO deploys trained observers to document forced evictions, police violence, and rights violations against people on the move in Northern France.

↗
MEMBERSHIP
Abolish Frontex
Pan-European Campaign

A campaign coalition of over 150 organisations demanding the abolition of Frontex, the European border and coast guard agency, and an end to the violent border regime it enforces across Europe.

↗

Support our work

Our investigations are built on the slow, careful work of documenting harm. Every contribution directly supports our research, our partnerships and our capacity to build evidence for legal accountability. If you would rather give by bank transfer, contact us at info@parallax.ngo.

Make a donation Refund policy Donations are voluntary gifts and are normally non-refundable. If something has gone wrong, for example a duplicate payment, the wrong amount, or a payment you did not authorise, email us at info@parallax.ngo and we will refund it to your original payment method.

Privacy Policy

Last updated: 03 July 2026

Parallax Investigations Ltd is a non-profit company limited by guarantee (company no. 16887325), registered in England and Wales. We are the controller of the personal information described here.

Registered office: 86–90 Paul Street, London, United Kingdom, EC2A 4NE.

What we collect and why

Donations - Your name, email, and the amount and date of your gift; your postal address or a message if you add one; and the account name and reference if you pay by bank transfer. We use this to process and acknowledge your donation and to keep proper financial records. Your card details go straight to Stripe on its hosted payment page, so we never see or store them. Basis: our legitimate interest in administering donations, and our legal duty to keep accounting records.

Volunteering and enquiries - Your name, contact details, and whatever you choose to tell us. We use it to reply to you and to manage your involvement. Basis: our legitimate interests.

Updates - Our newsletter runs on Substack. If you subscribe, Substack holds your details and we use them only to send you updates. Basis: your consent. You can unsubscribe at any time through Substack.

Website - We collect no personal data from visitors to our website, which uses no cookies and no tracking.

Sharing and transfers

We share your information only where needed: with Stripe (payments), our bank, Substack (updates), our accountants, and public authorities where the law requires. We never sell or rent it. Some of these providers, namely Stripe and Substack, may process data outside the UK, under safeguards recognised by UK data protection law.

How long we keep it

Financial records for six years; enquiry and volunteer records for as long as we need them; Substack subscribers until you unsubscribe.

Your rights

You can ask us to give you a copy of your data, correct it, delete it, restrict or object to its use, or transfer it, and you can withdraw consent at any time. Email info@parallax.ngo and we will respond within one month.

Complaints

Please contact us first at info@parallax.ngo. We will investigate your complaint or issue and respond within 14 days. You can also complain to the Information Commissioner’s Office at ico.org.uk or on 0303 123 1113.

Changes

We may update this notice. The date above shows the current version.
